source_file: [Day 14] Adventure Kit; 30 Days Lost in Space (English_ASR).srt
full_text: Good morning Intrepid Explorer it's time for our final day of our third week together and I do hope you're as excited as I am today we have a delightful rendevu with our former crew members the RGB LED we'll be integrating it into our setup and taking our control panel from functional to Fabulous today we'll be Transforming Our buzzer simple beep into an expressive and nuanced system of auditory feedback not only that but we're going to coordinate it with our RGB led to provide clear visual cues depending on your PIN input think of it as a form of immediate feedback when you successfully input your PIN we will immerse our spaceship in a comforting green light on the contrary if your input fails we will be met with a piercing red light as a sign of incorrect input green for Go Red For No it's so Universal that even a Celestial body could understand since there are no new components today we will be diving head first into the coating and wiring ready to create a sophisticated and intelligent spaceship let's get started all right time for some wiring if you remember how we set up our RGB LED last week you're already one step ahead today all we need to do is to merge that RGB setup with our current control panel configuration think of it as a bit of a Celestial or I guess aquatic engineering we're taking two previously independent systems and merging them into one harmonious unit it's quite beautiful isn't it we're building a network of devices that can interact and coordinate with each other in a most splendidly efficient manner just remember to ensure your wires don't look like a tangled seaweed cluster we need our circuits clear and our paths unobstructed once you've achieved this glorious Fusion of wires and components we can shift gears and explore the vast depths of coating buckle up it's about to get exciting all right my Fearless bottom dwelling mechanic it's time layout today's game plan here's what we're aiming to do step one we want to bathe the RGB in an emerald green glow when our system is either unlocked or a correct password has been punched in contrarily let it flare up with fiery Red Rage when an erroneous input infiltrates the system step two let's turn that buzzer into our personal symfony composing Melodies of success and failure as as needed step three moreover we'll make that buzzer emit a Brisk beep every time a button is pressed during the pin input sequence while we're at it why not manipulate the rgb's Hue to shades of yellow or blue this way we can visually signal that we're in the midst of pin input it's clever no I've been tinkering with a plan and I've decided that the most efficient and organized way to handle all this is by developing three new functions one to Express success one to denote error and one to indicate ongoing pin input plus we'll breathe life into the RGB LED color function from last week by now creating new functions should be as easy as breathing for you my dear space farer as for our friendly buzzer let's make it sing by using the tone function a high-pitch note will be our anthem of Victory while a low solemn tone will signify defeat the bones of our code will remain as sturdy as they were yesterday don't forget to add a tiny update at the top to Define our LED pins as usual I've crafted a line by line explanation of today's code and added it to your mission resources while it builds on what we did yesterday I highly recommend giving a look over your survival does hinge on understanding this after all the days sure are flying by Explorer now seems like a great time to grant me a welld deserved break as you review some instruction material that I have gathered cashed on your computer as you know I've also added this to your mission resources in case future you needs a bit more assistance hello everybody and welcome back to day 14 of the 30 days loss in space adventure kit code explanations today we're going to be building off of day 13 by enhancing the wiring in a way that we can add in an RGB led to our circuit to give us not only the audio feedback that we had yesterday after button inputs we will also be getting a visual glowing rgp indicator light you'll notice though that the wiring diagram is going to be a little bit different the reason why this is the case is because since the RGB LED requires pulse width modulation pins we had to modify the wiring diagram to make sure that we give the pulse with modulation pins to the RGB LED and then give the rest of the pins to the devices that don't require it the only new Aro concept introduced or documented in this lesson is going to just basically be some complex logic with multiple devices other than that there's not going to be anything new taught in this lesson just taking what we did yesterday and making it slightly more advanced since we've already covered the major majority of what's here at the top R we know. Keypad setup library from two days ago we got the pin length is four we talked about how we want to make the pins and then we obviously made the button Tod array we have the column and row pins remember these are changed these are not the same pins because we need to move the pins so that we could give the pulse with modulation ones to the RGB LED create the keypad setting the buzzer on pin 12 which is a new pin and the only really new thing at the top is right here we have constant red pin green pin and blue pin on pins 9 10 and 11 because those all have pulsewidth modulation capabilities at the beginning of setup we're using the pinMode function and we're going to be setting as normal LEDs or outputs so we're just setting each of those pins to be an output pin on this next line here since we start off is locked we're initially going to display every red color remember display color this function that we created before red Value Green value blue value green and blue are zero and then red is 128 so we're going to display red just to say hey we're currently locked out then of course just like we've already talked about in the previous lessons begin talking the serial Monitor and then we have the option to either create a new password or access the system jumping straight down to the logic of loop which is where it would go in the flow of code a lot of this is going to look the exact same as before we did add a couple of good extra additional functions though you'll notice here we have a function here called give input feedback give input feedback give input feedback this is located in a few different areas of the code and instead of what we did before which was hard coding each single time hey I want to play a tone of this Hertz rate every single time we simply turned it into a function so now if we ever want to change that tone or whatever we don't have to do it in 50 places instead we can just call this function give input feedback first we're going to turn off the LED just for a brief moment then we're going to have the tone for 2/10 of a second play a buzzer sound a buzzing sound just to give input feedback to know hey a button was pressed then we're going to display the color and restore the blue LED that should be on the display we do have different couple of colors that we're going to be using throughout this program the first one is red and that means you're currently locked you're locked and you're not doing anything you're not trying to input a new password it's not trying to unlock the system it's just currently locked and currently just sitting there you can think of it as like the lock screen on your computer where before you've actually gone to actually move your mouse or press the keyboard when it's in a moment where it's thinking to itself or doing something for example where when we're unlocking the console we're going to have blue light be the status light for hey you are currently doing something in this case either changing the password or trying to unlock the system all of this should be the exact same as what was said mentioned yesterday so I'm going to just glance over it and for right now down here below we have the same everything's almost the same except we do have one more here which is the input feedback is what we call here just in instead of uh CH doing the tone uh manually now down below we're actually going to be creating a new PIN color that we want to use in this case the pin color that we want to use is going to be yellow if you're entering a new pin and as you go down into the section where you're actually inputting the pin you'll notice we have the function give input feedback we didn't accidentally want to change it to uh blue at the end of every single time so at the end of of every time that we call this function here to give the input feedback which just makes a buzzer noise we're also going to override the color here so that it is a yellow LED once again every time we go to the loop now let's say that just for example you've successfully done all of that and the pin gets successful we're going to have another function very very similar to give input feedback which is give success feedback and it basically plays a tada sound and makes a green light and as you go down here we have d give success feedback we also have the function give error feedback which is another one that's called every once in a while that basically turns it to red and also plays a different tone to let you know hey you typed something Mis pressed something something went wrong that's essentially all it does then we also have the function display color which is the exact same I'm sure you remember from the RGB LED lesson from a while back it basically just says hey how what intensity do you want to set the red blue and green to and then does so so accordingly on the correct pins but other than that in terms of most the logic for validate pin all this other stuff we have the give error feedback we have give success feedback give input feedback besides these new little things that we're adding here everything is basically the same logic wise is what we went over yesterday so I don't really have to go over it in too too much detail the nice thing about having all these functions here give input give success and give error feedback is that instead of manually coding them into a bunch of different places in the code like here if we just did this tone buzzer pin 320 and then this in like a bunch of different places we can just give error feedback and we know like hey this is supposed to be an error right here it's we're reading through the code instead of saying this is a tone with this pin but I don't know why they're playing the tone it gives it better readability it's easier to go in if you want to change the tone to be something else let's just say you want to make it a Zelda sound effect where it's like d you can change that to be anywhere you want to instead of changing it in 60 places you change it in one good code readability good code reusability um and that's why we did it that way and with that that should be it for day 14 thank you guys so much welcome to the day 14 wiring example video if you've studied our plans for today you'll realize that we are using a lot of what we developed on day 13 but adding extra feedback both Visual and audible the red green blue LED will give us a colorcoded status indication and the buzzer will provide us in addition to keypress sounds special tones to indicate password success or failure now we'll be returning to these indicators in a minute but first let's have a look at the wiring needed for today's project now ideally it would be great if we could just leave the keypad setup intact from day 13 and simply add on the needed LED wiring unfortunately though we can't really do that because the LED can't use just any of the hero output pins recall that our RGB LED works by pulse width modulation and only some of the pins those whose number numers are preceded on the board by a Tilda only those pins support pwm in our day 13 Circuit there is only one pwm pin left unused but we need three of them to run the LED this means that we'll need to relocate some of our keypad wiring in order to free up pins 9 10 and 11 for use by our LED fortunately we can accomplish this by moving only one of our keypad wires the uppermost wire of our ribbon cable is the one that needs to move it gets moved from its original position of pin 9 which was a pwm pin up to the right to pin 13 and we're also going to reassign and use pin 12 which is available as the line with this yellow wire right here to drive our buzzer you should check the software sketch to make sure that these new assignments that we've made are reflected in the software sketch now that we have pins 9 10 and 11 on the hero available we go ahead and hook those up to the blue blue the green and the red channels of our LED through current limiting resistors as we usually do so this is pretty much just as we've set up the LED before finally with the ground connections connected here's the ground connection for the LED here's the ground connection for the buzzer so with those ground connections complete through the ground bus that we've set up that's all the hardware connections that we need to verify for this project so now let's quickly review what we expect to see and to hear when we run the sketch looking at what first happens when we enter setup the status color is red which we use to indicate that the system is locked once the loop function begins different colors and sounds will be produced depending upon the situation for example when the system is waiting for the current password to be entered the LED is blue so the blue indicates that it's waiting for a password entry on the other hand when it's waiting for a new password to be entered the color changes to Yellow so we'll watch out for that cue when the password is correctly entered then the LED shines green so that's our success indication anytime an incorrect password is subsequently entered then the LED goes back to Red Again by the way if you're paying close attention during all of this you might notice that the LED blinks out momentarily whenever we press one of the keys this it turns out is by Design as you can see if you carefully read the code for say Play Input tone or any of the other sound generating functions in our software sketch now this is a little bit of an obscure detail but we need to do this because as it turns out the pwm and tone functions both use the same hero resources and so it can be problematic to try using them at the same time so we avoid all that by simply turning off the LED while we're playing a sound so again that's just a small detail but uh in case you were wondering now you have the explanation well let's get on with it and run the sketch and see just how well these things work for us so moving over now to the IDE we'll go ahead and load the sketch and upload is complete and we can see that we have the red LED indicating that the system is locked and the first thing that we need to do is set a new password and to set a new password we'll have to enter the old default password which we'll remember is 000000 so pressing star key we see the LED blinked off and came back on Blue indicating that it's prompting us to enter the password which will do 0 0 0 0 and we get the success tone that indicates that we've successfully unlocked the system and we get the yellow LED indicating that we're being prompted to enter a new password so let's enter a new password let's try H 9512 okay we got a success tone and we got green which indicates that the system is unlocked and we've successfully changed the password all right so now that we're in and we have a new password we can try out a few things and make sure that it responds as we expect it to let's try re-entering the system but let's intentionally give it the wrong password so I'm going to hit the pound key and I get blue which means ready to enter password so let me enter 1 2 3 4 which is wrong okay as soon as I enter a wrong character I get the red LED and the the failure tone I could try it again and I'll start with a correct sequence so I'll start with 95 but now I'll give the wrong character for the next one I'll put in a three and that failed as well so now let's go ahead and enter the correct password so pressing the pound key then 9 5 1 2 and success and I'm back to green again so it looks like the functions as we've expected them to work the sound functions and the LED light functions are uh working as expected so it looks like our enhanced security interface is working pretty well now so looking ahead after you've finished creative day 15 we'll be back here on day 16 to tackle a whole new output display device and a new set of design challenges so we will see you then well look at you fish food we've reached the end of day 14 and may I say you've done spectacularly I'm impressed no astonished at the pace at which you're conquering these obstacles halfway through our ship repairs and you're still firing on all thrusters my digital heart if I had one would be brimming with Pride it's remarkable isn't it how you've been able to adapt and learn navigating through Uncharted territories of tech Wizardry all in a hostile environment millions of miles from home from securing the control panel to now giving it Visual and auditory feedback you've become a force to be reckoned with in Space Engineering rest now weary traveler you've earned your sea legs let the fish whisper Tales of your endeavors across the ocean as you recharge your human batteries and remember the tides wait for No One tomorrow we swim onward congratulations on reaching this Milestone it's not every day one gets to say they're halfway done repairing a spaceship stranded in Coral now is it
